statcast.definition: A tracking system installed in every MLB stadium in 2015 that uses radar and high-speed cameras to measure the physical properties of every play: ball speed, launch angle, spin rate, player positioning, and more. The data it produces forms the basis of modern baseball analytics.

exit velocity.definition: The speed of the ball off the bat, measured in miles per hour. A higher exit velocity means the ball was hit harder. The best hitters in baseball average 92–95 mph or above.

launch angle.definition: The vertical angle at which the ball leaves the bat. Zero degrees is a flat line drive. Negative angles are ground balls. Between roughly 10 and 30 degrees is the range where home runs and extra-base hits are most common. Above 45 degrees, the ball goes nearly straight up and becomes an infield pop-up.

barrel.definition: A ball hit with enough exit velocity (at least 98 mph) and at a launch angle (roughly 26–30 degrees) that Statcast classifies as nearly certain to produce an extra-base hit. Barrels almost always result in extra-base hits.

barrel rate.definition: The percentage of a hitter's balls in play that qualify as barrels. Elite hitters have barrel rates of 15–20% or higher; league average is around 6–8%.

attack angle.definition: The slope of the bat's path through the hitting zone at the moment of contact, measured in degrees. A flat swing is near zero degrees; a steep uppercut is 15–20 degrees. Attack angle directly influences launch angle: a flat swing produces ground balls and low line drives, while an upward swing sends the ball airborne.

xslg.definition: A Statcast metric estimating what a player's slugging percentage should have been based on contact quality — exit velocity and launch angle — independent of where balls actually landed. Differences between xSLG and actual slugging percentage reflect luck, good or bad.

expected slugging.definition: A Statcast metric estimating what a player's slugging percentage should have been based on contact quality — exit velocity and launch angle — independent of where balls actually landed. Differences between xSLG and actual slugging percentage reflect luck, good or bad.

bat speed.definition: The speed of the bat barrel at the moment of contact, measured in miles per hour. Faster bat speed produces more power and gives a hitter more time to read a pitch before committing to a swing. Elite bat speed is generally 75 mph or above. It tends to decline as players age through their thirties.

hard-hit rate.definition: The percentage of a hitter's balls in play struck at 95 mph or harder. One of the more stable contact quality metrics from season to season.

ground ball rate.definition: The percentage of a hitter's balls in play that go on the ground. Power hitters generally have lower ground ball rates, as home runs require the ball to be in the air. League average is around 43–45%; rates above 50% tend to suppress power production.

fly ball rate.definition: The percentage of a hitter's balls in play that go in the air. Home runs require fly balls, so hitters with higher fly ball rates combined with good exit velocity tend to produce more power. League average is around 35–38%.

iso.definition: Slugging percentage minus batting average. By removing singles from the calculation, ISO measures only extra-base production. A .200 ISO is considered strong; .250 or above is elite.

isolated power.definition: Slugging percentage minus batting average. By removing singles from the calculation, ISO measures only extra-base production. A .200 ISO is considered strong; .250 or above is elite.

adp.definition: The average spot at which a player is selected across a large sample of fantasy baseball drafts. ADP reflects the market consensus on a player's value heading into a season.

slugging percentage.definition: Total bases divided by at-bats. A single counts as 1, a double as 2, a triple as 3, a home run as 4. A .500 slugging percentage is considered excellent; league average is usually around .400.

batting average.definition: Hits divided by at-bats. A .300 average is considered excellent; .250 is roughly league average. All hits count equally regardless of the type of contact that produced them.

fantasy baseball.definition: A game in which participants draft real MLB players and compete based on those players' actual statistics during the season. Scoring systems vary, but most formats include traditional stats such as home runs, batting average, RBIs, stolen bases, wins, and ERA.

rbi.definition: A counting stat crediting a batter when his plate appearance results in a run scoring. RBI totals are heavily influenced by lineup position and the number of baserunners a hitter encounters, making them as much a measure of opportunity as of individual production.

rbis.definition: A counting stat crediting a batter when his plate appearance results in a run scoring. RBI totals are heavily influenced by lineup position and the number of baserunners a hitter encounters.

strikeout rate.definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances that end in a strikeout. Elite contact hitters are generally below 15%; aggressive power hitters often exceed 25–30%. A high strikeout rate reduces batting average and limits the number of balls put in play.

k%.definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances that end in a strikeout. Elite contact hitters are generally below 15%; aggressive power hitters often exceed 25–30%.

walk rate.definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances that result in a walk. A measure of plate discipline — the ability to identify and lay off pitches outside the strike zone. Walk rate is one of the most stable year-to-year statistics in a hitter's profile.

bb%.definition: The percentage of a batter's plate appearances that result in a walk. A measure of plate discipline — the ability to identify and lay off pitches outside the strike zone.

box score.definition: The traditional statistical summary of a baseball game: at-bats, hits, runs, RBIs, home runs, and so on. Box score statistics record outcomes but not the quality of contact that produced them.

fast-swing rate.definition: The percentage of a batter's swings in which the bat reaches 75 mph or more. A high fast-swing rate indicates consistent power generation. Decline in fast-swing rate is among the earlier measurable indicators of aging in a power hitter.

spring training.definition: The pre-season practice period in February and March when teams play exhibition games before Opening Day. Results-based statistics from spring training — batting average, ERA, home runs — are generally unreliable due to small sample sizes and uneven competition. Batted-ball shape metrics, which reflect swing mechanics rather than outcomes, carry more signal in this context.

line drive.definition: A ball hit sharply and relatively flat, typically between 10 and 25 degrees of launch angle. Line drives have the highest batting average of any batted ball type.

breaking ball.definition: A pitch that curves or breaks laterally as it travels to the plate. Curveballs and sliders are the most common types. Breaking balls are thrown slower than fastballs and are designed to change location at the last moment.

extra-base hit.definition: Any hit on which the batter reaches second base or beyond: a double, triple, or home run. Extra-base hits are the primary driver of run production and the main quantity measured by slugging percentage.

projection systems.definition: Statistical models that use a player's historical performance to forecast his production in the upcoming season. Common systems include Steamer, ZiPS, and ATC. Projections form the basis of most preseason fantasy rankings and draft prices.

iffb%.definition: The percentage of a hitter's fly balls that land in the infield. A ball hit above roughly 45 degrees of launch angle goes nearly vertical, stays in the infield, and is an automatic out. IFFB% is not influenced by defensive positioning, making it a direct measure of swing path quality. League average is around 10–12%.

ops.definition: On-base percentage added to slugging percentage. A .800 OPS is considered solid; .900 or above is excellent. OPS rewards both the ability to reach base and the ability to hit for extra bases, but weights them equally despite their different run values.

woba.definition: A measure of a hitter's offensive value per plate appearance that weights each outcome — walk, single, double, home run — by its actual run value. A .320 wOBA is roughly league average; .370 or above is excellent.

xwoba.definition: A Statcast metric estimating what a hitter's wOBA should have been based on contact quality — exit velocity and launch angle — independent of where balls actually landed. A large gap between xwOBA and actual wOBA indicates results that differed significantly from contact quality.

babip.term: BABIP (Batting Average on Balls in Play)
babip.definition: Batting average calculated only on balls put in play, excluding home runs and strikeouts. League average is around .300. BABIP fluctuates more than most hitting statistics and tends to regress toward a player's career baseline over time.

wrc+.definition: A park- and league-adjusted measure of a hitter's total offensive production, scaled so that 100 is league average. A wRC+ of 120 means a hitter produced 20 percent more offense than an average player in the same conditions; 80 means 20 percent below average.

hr/fb.term: HR/FB Rate (Home Run to Fly Ball Rate)
hr/fb.definition: The percentage of a hitter's fly balls that result in home runs. League average is around 13 percent; the best power hitters sustain 20 to 25 percent over a full season. HR/FB rate fluctuates considerably from year to year and is among the less stable metrics in a hitter's profile.
